title: “Furthermore, monks, one might see that woman — sick, suffering, gravely ill, lying in her own urine and feces, needing others to lift her, needing…
content: “Furthermore, monks, one might see that woman — sick, suffering, gravely ill, lying in her own urine and feces, needing others to lift her, needing others to lay her down. What do you think, monks? Has her former charm and beauty disappeared and peril become evident?” “Yes, venerable sir.” “Thus, monks, is the peril in the case of forms.”

title: “Monks, I will teach you nine things rooted in craving. Listen and pay close attention. And what, monks, are the nine things rooted in craving?…
content: “Monks, I will teach you nine things rooted in craving. Listen and pay close attention. And what, monks, are the nine things rooted in craving? Dependent on craving, seeking arises; dependent on seeking, gain arises; dependent on gain, decision arises; dependent on decision, desire and lust arise; dependent on desire and lust, attachment arises; dependent on attachment, possessiveness arises; dependent on possessiveness, guarding arises; dependent on guarding, the taking up of sticks and swords, quarrels, disputes, arguments, slander, false speech, and many evil, unwholesome states arise. These, monks, are the nine things rooted in craving.”

title: “Hearing the Buddha’s words, The wise monk fully understands the body, Sees it as it really is. As it is below, so above, As above,…
content:
“Hearing the Buddha’s words,
The wise monk fully understands the body,
Sees it as it really is.

As it is below, so above,
As above, so below —
Abandoning desire for the body inside and out,

The wise monk abandons desire and lust.
He attains the deathless, peace,
The constant state of Nibbāna.

This two-footed, impure, foul-smelling body
Filled with various rotting corpses, oozing —
Yet it is preserved and honored.

Who would think to exalt this body,
Or despise another —
Except one who is blind?”

title: “I do not see any other thing, monks, through which unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline, as intimacy with unwholesome states and…
content: “I do not see any other thing, monks, through which unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline, as intimacy with unwholesome states and non-intimacy with wholesome states. Through intimacy with unwholesome states and non-intimacy with wholesome states, monks, unarisen unwholesome states arise and arisen wholesome states decline.”

title: “Knowing this body to be like a clay pot, Fortifying this mind like a city, Fight Māra with the sword of wisdom, Guard your conquest,…
content:
“Knowing this body to be like a clay pot,
Fortifying this mind like a city,
Fight Māra with the sword of wisdom,
Guard your conquest, and be free of attachment.”

“Before long, alas, this body
Will lie on the ground,
Cast aside, devoid of consciousness,
Like a useless log.”

title: “Monks, this saṃsāra is without discoverable beginning. A first point cannot be discerned of beings wandering and roaming on, shrouded by ignorance and bound by…
content: “Monks, this saṃsāra is without discoverable beginning. A first point cannot be discerned of beings wandering and roaming on, shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving. It is possible, monks, that the great ocean might dry up, be exhausted, and cease to exist. But I say that beings shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving, wandering and roaming on, will not have made an end of suffering. It is possible, monks, that Sineru, the king of mountains, might be destroyed and cease to exist. But I say that beings shrouded by ignorance … will not have made an end of suffering. It is possible, monks, that the great earth might be destroyed and cease to exist. But I say that beings shrouded by ignorance and bound by craving, wandering and roaming on, will not have made an end of suffering!”

title: “Monks, those ascetics or brahmins who do not know as it actually is the gratification as gratification, the peril as peril, the escape as escape…
content: “Monks, those ascetics or brahmins who do not know as it actually is the gratification as gratification, the peril as peril, the escape as escape in the case of forms — they will certainly not understand forms as they actually are, nor can they lead others to a similar position, namely, understanding forms as they actually are — such a thing is not possible. Monks, those ascetics or brahmins who know as it actually is the gratification as gratification, the peril as peril, the escape as escape in the case of forms — they will certainly understand forms as they actually are, and they can lead others to a similar position, namely, understanding forms as they actually are — such a thing is possible.”

title: “O brahmin, strive to cut the stream, Abandon sensual pleasures. Knowing the destruction of formations, You will be the one who knows the Unmade.” “By…
content:
“O brahmin, strive to cut the stream,
Abandon sensual pleasures.
Knowing the destruction of formations,
You will be the one who knows the Unmade.”

“By always dwelling in two Dhammas,
One reaches the further shore.
That brahmin, wise,
Cuts off all fetters.”

“One who has no near shore, no far shore,
No both shores,
Free from suffering, unbound —
Him I call a brahmin.”

“Meditating, free from dust,
Duty done, without defilements,
Having attained the highest goal —
Him I call a brahmin.”

title: “Venerable sir, ‘stain’ is called a stain. What term is synonymous with what is called ‘stain’?” “Venerable sir, evil, unwholesome states, the realm of sensual…
content:
“Venerable sir, ‘stain’ is called a stain. What term is synonymous with what is called ‘stain’?”
“Venerable sir, evil, unwholesome states, the realm of sensual desire, are synonymous with what is called ‘stain.’”

title: “Monks, sensual thought is blind, causes blindness, causes lack of knowledge, leads to the destruction of wisdom, partakes of distress, does not lead to Nibbāna.…
content:
“Monks, sensual thought is blind, causes blindness, causes lack of knowledge, leads to the destruction of wisdom, partakes of distress, does not lead to Nibbāna. Monks, ill-will thought is blind … harmful thought is blind, causes blindness, causes lack of knowledge, leads to the destruction of wisdom, partakes of distress, does not lead to Nibbāna.

Three thoughts of wholesome kind
One should frequently reflect upon —
Three unwholesome thoughts should be abandoned, turned away from.
That one surely restrains
The conduct of thoughts.

As rain settles the dust,
So by calming thoughts with the mind,
One here and now attains
The peaceful path of peace.”
